title: Identity in Christ
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Identity in Christ
summary: The New Testament declares that union with Christ creates a new identity — chosen, adopted, redeemed, and sealed — that supersedes every other label or status.
body:
Paul's most radical identity statement comes in Galatians: there is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus [verse:gal.3.28]. In Christ, the categories that define and divide humanity are relativized — not erased, but no longer ultimate.

Believers are described as a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession [verse:1pet.2.9]. Peter uses collective language deliberately — identity in Christ is never purely individual; it is communal.

Paul tells the Corinthians that anyone in Christ is a new creation; the old has passed away, the new has come [verse:2cor.5.17]. This is ontological, not merely motivational. Something has actually changed.

The metaphor of adoption is central. Believers receive the Spirit of adoption by whom they cry 'Abba, Father' [verse:rom.8.15-16]. The identity is relational — not employee of God but child of God, with all the intimacy that implies.

Ephesians layers identity statements: chosen before the foundation of the world, predestined for adoption, redeemed through his blood, sealed with the promised Holy Spirit [verse:eph.1.3-14]. Each term adds a dimension. Identity in Christ is not a single idea but a rich, multifaceted reality.

When the world asks 'Who are you?' Scripture answers: you are God's, and that is enough.

verses.annotation: In Christ, human divisions are relativized — unity transcends categories.
New creation — not self-improvement but ontological transformation.
Chosen, royal, holy, possessed — communal identity language.
Spirit of adoption — identity as child of God, not servant.
Layered identity: chosen, adopted, redeemed, sealed.
Your life is hidden with Christ in God — identity secured in the most protected place.

scholars.quote: Schreiner argues that Paul's 'new creation' in 2 Corinthians 5:17 is not merely personal renewal but participation in the new age inaugurated by Christ's resurrection — identity change is embedded in cosmic change.
scholars.source: Paul, Apostle of God's Glory in Christ (Schreiner, 2001)

scholars.quote: Fee emphasizes that the Spirit of adoption in Romans 8:15 produces experiential intimacy — 'Abba' is not a formal title but a child's address to a trusted father, indicating that identity in Christ is relational before it is doctrinal.
Beale reads Ephesians 1:3-14 as an identity charter — the passage establishes the believer's status so comprehensively that no subsequent experience can add to or subtract from it.
